Question: Who were the two wives of Elkanah?
Response: The wives of Elkanah were Hannah and Peninnah.

Question: How many children did Hannah have at that time?
Response: At that time Hannah had no children.

Question: Why did Elkanah give twice as much meat to Hannah?
Response: He gave a double portion to Hannah because he loved her.

Question: Why did Hannah’s adversary provoke her?
Response: She provoked Hannah in order to make her upset, because Yahweh had closed her womb.

Question: What did Hannah do because she was so sorrowful about not having children?
Response: She went to the house of Yahweh, and she cried very sorrowfully as she prayed to Yahweh.

Question: What did Hannah vow to Yahweh?
Response: Hannah vowed that if Yahweh would give her a son, she would give him to Yahweh and they would never cut his hair.

Question: Because Hannah was speaking to Yahweh from her heart, what did Eli the priest think about her actions?
Response: Eli saw Hannah’s lips moving but did not hear her voice, so he thought she was drunk.

Question: What did Hannah assure Eli she was doing?
Response: Hannah assured Eli that she was not drunk but was talking to Yahweh because someone made her feel very badly.

Question: What did Eli say to Hannah that caused her to go on her way, eat, and no longer feel sad?
Response: Eli told her to go in peace and said that he hoped the God of Israel would grant her request.

Question: When Hannah conceived and gave birth to a son, what did she name him?
Response: Hannah named her son Samuel because she had requested him from Yahweh.

Question: Why did Hannah not go with her husband to the house of Yahweh for the yearly sacrifice?
Response: She did not go because she was waiting until her son was weaned.

Question: What offerings did Hannah bring with her when she brought her son to the house of Yahweh?
Response: Hannah brought three bulls, an ephah of flour, and a skin of wine.

Question: What did Hannah present to Yahweh?
Response: Hannah presented her son to Yahweh for as long as he would live.

Question: Why did Hannah laugh at her enemies?
Response: Hannah laughed because she rejoiced in Yahweh’s salvation.

Question: Why does Hannah say that people should not boast or speak arrogantly?
Response: People should not boast or speak arrogantly because Yahweh is a God of knowledge, and by him actions are weighed.

Question: In Hannah’s song, who is the one who is unable to have more children?
Response: The woman who had many children before is not able to have any more.

Question: Who decides death and life, or who goes down to Sheol or is raised up?
Response: Yahweh is the one who decides death and life, and who causes people to go down to Sheol or to come up from there.

Question: Who makes people poor or rich?
Response: Yahweh makes people poor, and he makes them rich.

Question: Who raises up the poor and lifts the needy to sit in a seat of honor?
Response: Yahweh raises them up to sit in a seat of honor.

Question: Who does Hannah say will protect the feet of his faithful people and silence the wicked in darkness?
Response: Hannah says that Yahweh will guard the feet of his faithful people and will silence the wicked.

Question: Who will judge the ends of the earth and give strength to his king?
Response: Yahweh will judge the ends of the earth and give strength to his king.

Question: What child served Yahweh in the presence of Eli the priest?
Response: Samuel served Yahweh in the presence of Eli the priest.

Question: Whose sons were wicked men who did not know Yahweh?
Response: Eli the priest’s sons did very bad things.

Question: What was the custom of the priests with the people at the time of sacrifice?
Response: The custom was for the priest’s servant to come with a three-pronged fork while the meat was boiling and take whatever the fork brought up for the priest.

Question: What evil thing did Eli’s sons have their servant do?
Response: They had their servant demand raw meat for the priest before the fat was burned as an offering to Yahweh, and had him take it by force if the person refused.

Question: Why was the sin of Eli’s sons so great?
Response: Their sin was great before Yahweh because the men were treating very disrespectfully the offerings that people were bringing to Yahweh.

Question: What would Samuel’s mother make for him from year to year to clothe him?
Response: She would make him a small robe each year.

Question: How would Eli bless Elkanah and his wife?
Response: Eli blessed them by asking Yahweh to give them more children from Hannah.

Question: What was Yahweh’s response to Eli’s prayer for Samuel’s parents to have more children?
Response: Yahweh helped Hannah, and she bore three more sons and two daughters.

Question: What did Eli say to his sons when he heard evil reports about them?
Response: Eli told his sons that the report he was hearing from Yahweh’s people about their evil deeds was not good. He told them to stop doing what they were doing.

Question: Why did Eli’s sons not listen to his voice?
Response: Eli’s sons did not listen to his voice because Yahweh desired to make them die.

Question: Who grew up and was good in the sight of Yahweh and also with men?
Response: Samuel grew up and was good in the sight of Yahweh and with men.

Question: Who told Eli that Yahweh chose Eli’s ancestors out of all the tribes of Israel to be Yahweh’s priest?
Response: A man of God told Eli that Yahweh had chosen his ancestors.

Question: How had Eli and his sons scorned Yahweh’s sacrifices and offerings?
Response: They scorned the sacrifices and offerings by honoring his sons above Yahweh and fattening themselves on the best parts of offerings.

Question: What did Yahweh tell Eli would happen to the men in his family?
Response: Yahweh told Eli that all the men born in his family would die young.

Question: What did Yahweh say would be a sign to Eli that his prophecy would come true?
Response: The sign would be that both of his sons would die on the same day.

Question: Who will walk before Yahweh’s faithful priest always?
Response: Yahweh has chosen another man who will serve him faithfully and continually be a helper to the king whom Yahweh will appoint.

Question: Why will everyone left in Eli’s family come and bow down to the faithful priest?
Response: They will ask him to allow them to work with the other priests so that they can have food to eat.

Question: What was Samuel’s reply when he heard Yahweh call to him as he was lying down?
Response: Samuel replied that he was ready to do whatever you ask.

Question: What did Eli tell Samuel to do when Samuel ran to him?
Response: Eli said that he did not call Samuel and told him to go lie down again.

Question: What had not yet been revealed to Samuel?
Response: The word of Yahweh had not yet been revealed to Samuel.

Question: What did Eli realize after the third time that Samuel came to him?
Response: Eli realized that it was Yahweh who had been calling Samuel.

Question: What did Eli tell Samuel to say if he was called again?
Response: Eli told Samuel to say that Yahweh should speak, because his servant was listening.

Question: What did Yahweh say would happen to the ears of everyone when they heard what he was going to do?
Response: Yahweh said that the ears of everyone who heard it would tingle.

Question: What had Eli’s sons brought upon themselves?
Response: Eli’s sons had brought a curse upon themselves.

Question: What would never be atoned for by sacrifice or offering?
Response: The iniquity of Eli’s house would never be atoned for by sacrifice or offering.

Question: What was Samuel afraid to do in the morning after he opened the doors of the house of Yahweh?
Response: He was afraid to tell Eli about the vision.

Question: What did Samuel do after Eli told him that he must not hide from him the words that Yahweh had spoken?
Response: Samuel told him everything and hid nothing from him.

Question: What did all Israel from Dan to Beersheba know about Samuel?
Response: All Israel knew that Samuel was established as a prophet of Yahweh.

Question: What was the result of the battle between Israel and the Philistines?
Response: Israel was defeated by the Philistines.

Question: What did the elders of Israel decide to do so that they could be saved from the power of their enemies?
Response: The elders decided to bring the Box of the Covenant of Yahweh from Shiloh to be with them.

Question: Who was with the Box of the Covenant of God?
Response: The two sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, were there with the Box of the Covenant.

Question: What did the Philistines wonder when they heard the great shout from the people of Israel?
Response: The Philistines wondered about the meaning of the great shout in the camp of the Hebrews.

Question: Who did the Philistines say had come into the camp of the Hebrews?
Response: The Philistines said that a god had come into the camp.

Question: With what did the Philistines say that the gods of Israel had struck the Egyptians in the wilderness?
Response: They said that the gods had struck the Egyptians with every kind of plague.

Question: What happened to the two sons of Eli when the Box of God was taken Israel was struck and fled?
Response: The two sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, died.

Question: What did the whole city do when the man from the battle line entered the city and told the news?
Response: When the man told the news, the whole city cried out.

Question: What question did Eli ask when he heard the noise of the outcry?
Response: Eli asked what the sound of the tumult meant.

Question: What did the man tell Eli had happened to his sons and to the Box of God when Israel was defeated?
Response: The man told Eli that his two sons were dead and the Box of God had been taken.

Question: What was the injury that killed Eli when he fell over backward from his seat by the side of the gate?
Response: Eli broke his neck and he died.

Question: What happened when Phinehas’s pregnant wife heard that the Box of God was captured and her father-in-law and husband were dead?
Response: Phinehas’s wife knelt down and gave birth because her labor pains suddenly began.

Question: What name did Phinehas’s wife give to the baby and why did she give him that name?
Response: She named the baby Ichabod, saying that the glory had departed from Israel because the Box of God had been taken.

Question: In what position did the people find the idol Dagon the next day after the Philistines brought the Box of God into Dagon’s house?
Response: Dagon was found fallen face down on the ground before the Box of Yahweh.

Question: What remained of the idol Dagon the next morning after they had set him back in his place?
Response: Only the body of Dagon remained; his head and hands were lying cut off in the doorway.

Question: Why did the men of Ashdod say that the Box of the God of Israel should not stay with them?
Response: They said it should not stay because the hand of their God was severe against them and against their god Dagon.

Question: To what place did the Philistines move the Box of the God of Israel?
Response: The Philistines moved the Box to Gath.

Question: With what did Yahweh strike the men of the city of Gath?
Response: Yahweh struck them with tumors.

Question: What did the people of Ekron say the Box of the God of Israel would do to them?
Response: They said that it would kill them and their people.

Question: To what place did the people of Ekron ask the rulers to send the Box?
Response: They asked that the Box be sent back to its own place.

Question: To what place did the cry of the city go?
Response: The cry of the city went up to the heavens.

Question: For how long was the Box of Yahweh in the Philistines’ country?
Response: The Box was in the country of the Philistines for seven months.

Question: Who did the Philistines call to ask for advice on how the Box of Yahweh should be sent back to its own country?
Response: They called for the priests and the diviners to tell them how to send the Box back.

Question: What did the priests and diviners tell the Philistine people to send as a guilt offering to the God of Israel?
Response: They told the Philistines to send five gold tumors and five gold mice.

Question: Why did the priests and diviners say the people should not be stubborn like the Egyptians and Pharaoh were?
Response: Because when God dealt severely with the Egyptians and Pharaoh, they had to let Israel go.

Question: What animals did the priests and diviners tell the Philistines to tie to the cart for the Box of Yahweh?
Response: They told the Philistines to hitch two nursing cows to the cart.

Question: How would the Philistines know that it was Yahweh who had done this great harm to them?
Response: The Philistines would know it was Yahweh if the cows pulling the cart went on their own to Beth Shemesh.

Question: Where did the nursing cows go with the cart carrying the Box and the gold mice and tumors?
Response: The cows went straight in the direction of Beth Shemesh.

Question: What were the people of Beth Shemesh doing when they lifted up their eyes and saw the Box?
Response: They were harvesting their wheat in the valley.

Question: What did the people of Beth Shemesh do with the cows that had pulled the cart?
Response: They offered the cows as a burnt offering to Yahweh.

Question: Who took down the Box of Yahweh and the box that was with it?
Response: The Levites took down the Box of Yahweh and the box that was with it.

Question: What did the five rulers of the Philistines do when they saw what had happened?
Response: They returned that day to Ekron.

Question: What did the numbers of gold tumors and gold mice represent?
Response: The five gold tumors represented the people of their five cities, and the gold mice represented all the Philistine cities belonging to those rulers.

Question: Why did Yahweh strike some of the men of Beth Shemesh?
Response: Yahweh struck them because they looked into his Box.

Question: What did the messengers from Beth Shemesh ask the people of Kiriath Jearim to do?
Response: The messengers asked the people of Kiriath Jearim to come and take the Box of Yahweh to their city.

Question: What did the men of Kiriath Jearim do to Abinadab’s son, Eleazar, so that he could take care of the Box of Yahweh?
Response: They consecrated him to keep the Box of Yahweh.

Question: What did all the house of Israel do during the twenty years that the Box remained in Kiriath Jearim?
Response: During that time, the Israelite people were very sorry that Yahweh was not helping them anymore.

Question: What did Samuel tell the people of Israel that they needed to remove from among them for Yahweh to rescue them from the Philistines?
Response: Samuel told them that they needed to remove the foreign gods and the Ashtoreths from among them and serve Yahweh only.

Question: What did the people of Israel admit that they had done when they gathered at Mizpah?
Response: They admitted that they had sinned against Yahweh.

Question: Why did the people of Israel ask Samuel to call out to Yahweh for them?
Response: They were afraid because they heard that the rulers of the Philistines were coming to attack them.

Question: What did Yahweh do when Samuel offered a lamb as a burnt offering and cried out to him?
Response: Yahweh answered Samuel.

Question: What did Yahweh do that threw the Philistines into confusion as they drew near to attack Israel?
Response: Yahweh thundered with a great sound on that day against the Philistines.

Question: What did Samuel set up between Mizpah and Shen as a remembrance that Yahweh had helped them?
Response: Samuel took a stone and set it up.

Question: What happened to the towns that the Philistines had taken from Israel?
Response: They were recaptured by Israel.

Question: What did Samuel do as he went on a circuit to Bethel, Gilgal, Mizpah, and back to Ramah?
Response: Samuel judged Israel in all these places.

Question: In what ways did Samuel’s two sons, who were judges, not walk in the ways of their father?
Response: They were not honest like their father. They accepted bribes, and they did not make honest decisions about people’s disputes.

Question: What request did the elders of Israel make of Samuel?
Response: They asked Samuel to appoint a king to judge them, like all the other nations.

Question: What did Samuel do when he was displeased by the elders’ request to give them a king?
Response: Samuel prayed to Yahweh.

Question: Why did Yahweh tell Samuel to listen to the voice of the people?
Response: Yahweh told Samuel to listen because the people were not rejecting Samuel, but they were rejecting Yahweh from reigning over them.

Question: What warning did Yahweh tell Samuel to give to the people of Israel?
Response: Yahweh told Samuel to warn the people about the way the king would rule over them.

Question: For what did Samuel warn the people that a king would take their sons?
Response: Samuel warned that the king would take their sons to be officers and soldiers, to plow his fields and harvest his crops, and to make weapons of war.

Question: How did Samuel warn the people about what a king would do with their daughters and property?
Response: Samuel warned that the king would take their daughters to be perfumers, cooks, and bakers, and he would take the best of their fields and vineyards and olive groves to give to his attendants.

Question: How did Samuel warn the people about what a king would do with their servants and animals?
Response: Samuel warned that the king would take their servants, the best of their young men and donkeys, and a tenth of their flocks, and the people would become his servants.

Question: What did Samuel warn would happen when the people cried out because of the king they had chosen?
Response: Samuel said that Yahweh would not answer them on that day.

Question: How did the people of Israel respond to the warnings from Samuel?
Response: The people refused to listen and said they wanted a king over them so they could be like the other nations.

Question: What was Yahweh’s response when Samuel repeated the people’s words to him?
Response: Yahweh told Samuel to listen to their voice and appoint a king for them.

Question: What were the outstanding physical features of Saul, the son of Kish?
Response: Saul was a handsome young man who was taller than any of the other people from his shoulders upward.

Question: What was the outcome when Kish asked his son Saul to go and look for their lost donkeys?
Response: Saul took one of the servants and passed through many places without finding them.

Question: What did Saul tell his servant they should do when they came to the land of Zuph?
Response: Saul told his servant that they should go back so his father would not stop being concerned about the donkeys and become concerned about them.

Question: What different idea did the servant present to Saul?
Response: The servant suggested they should go to a man of God in the city who might tell them the way they should go.

Question: What did the servant have to give to the man of God so he would tell them which way to go?
Response: The servant had a fourth of a shekel of silver to give to the man of God.

Question: What did Saul and his servant ask the young women who were coming out to draw water?
Response: Saul and his servant asked if the seer was in the city.

Question: Why was the seer coming to the city on that day?
Response: The seer was coming to the city because the people were having a sacrifice at the high place, and he would bless the sacrifice.

Question: What did Yahweh reveal to Samuel about Saul’s role in Israel?
Response: Yahweh told Samuel that he was to anoint Saul as ruler over Israel, and Saul would save Yahweh’s people from the hand of the Philistines.

Question: What invitation did Samuel give to Saul after he revealed that he was the seer Saul was seeking?
Response: Samuel invited Saul to go up and eat with him at the high place.

Question: How did Samuel surprise Saul with what he told him?
Response: Samuel told him that all the desire of Israel was for him and his father’s house, even though Saul was from the least clan of the smallest tribe.

Question: What did Samuel tell the cook to serve to Saul?
Response: Samuel told the cook to serve Saul the thigh portion that had been set aside for him for the appointed time.

Question: What did Samuel say to Saul from the roof in the morning?
Response: Samuel told Saul to get up so he could send him on his way.

Question: Why did Samuel tell Saul to stand still for a while on the outskirts of the city?
Response: Samuel told Saul to stand still so Samuel could tell him the word of God.

Question: Why did Samuel pour a flask of oil on Saul’s head and kiss him?
Response: He did this because Yahweh had anointed Saul to be a ruler over his inheritance.

Question: What future event did Samuel share with Saul?
Response: Samuel told Saul that he would find two men near Rachel’s tomb who would tell him that the donkeys had been found.

Question: What did Samuel tell Saul would happen when he came to the oak of Tabor?
Response: Three men going up to God at Bethel would meet him and give him two loaves of bread, which he should take.

Question: What did Samuel tell Saul would happen when he came to Gibeah of God?
Response: The Spirit of Yahweh would rush upon him, he would prophesy with a group of prophets, and he would be changed into a different man.

Question: What instructions did Samuel give to Saul for later?
Response: Samuel told Saul to go down to Gilgal and wait seven days for Samuel to come and offer sacrifices.

Question: What happened when Saul turned his back to leave Samuel?
Response: When Saul turned to leave, God changed Saul’s inner being.

Question: Why did people wonder if Saul was one of the prophets?
Response: They saw him prophesying with the prophets and wondered how this had happened to the son of Kish.

Question: What did Saul tell his uncle when he was asked where he had gone?
Response: Saul told his uncle that when he and his servant could not find the donkeys, they went to Samuel.

Question: What did Saul not tell his uncle about what Samuel had said to him?
Response: Saul did not tell his uncle about the matter of the kingship.

Question: Why did Yahweh say that Israel had rejected him?
Response: Israel had rejected Yahweh because they had asked for a king to be set over them.

Question: What happened when Saul, the son of Kish, was chosen as the next king?
Response: They looked for Saul, but he could not be found.

Question: Where was Saul when he was chosen as the next king?
Response: Saul had hidden himself among the bags.

Question: What did Samuel say about the man whom Yahweh had chosen?
Response: Samuel said that there was no one like Saul among all the people.

Question: How were the customs of the kingship established and preserved?
Response: Samuel told the people the customs of the kingship, wrote them in a book, and placed the book before Yahweh.

Question: How did some wicked men show their dislike for Saul?
Response: Some wicked men despised Saul and did not bring him a gift.

Question: What was the response of Nahash the Ammonite to the request for a covenant from the men of Jabesh?
Response: He said he would make a covenant with them if he could gouge out all their right eyes and bring disgrace on Israel.

Question: How did the elders of Jabesh respond to Nahash’s proposal?
Response: They asked for seven days to send messengers throughout Israel to see if anyone would save them.

Question: Why were the people in Saul’s town of Gibeah weeping?
Response: They were weeping because they had heard the words of the men from Jabesh.

Question: How did Saul respond to the threat against Jabesh?
Response: The Spirit of God rushed upon him, he became very angry, and he persuaded the men of Israel and Judah to fight.

Question: What message did the messengers bring back to the men of Jabesh?
Response: They told the men of Jabesh that they would have deliverance the next day by the time the sun was hot.

Question: What did the men of Jabesh say to Nahash the Ammonite?
Response: They told Nahash that they would come out to him the next day, and he could do to them whatever seemed good to him.

Question: What was the outcome of the battle between Israel and the Ammonites?
Response: The people of Israel attacked and defeated the Ammonites, and the survivors were totally scattered.

Question: Why did Saul say that none of the men who opposed him would be put to death?
Response: Saul said no one would be put to death, because that day Yahweh had brought deliverance to Israel.

Question: Why did Samuel, Saul, and all the people of Israel go to Gilgal?
Response: They went to Gilgal to renew the kingship, make Saul king before Yahweh, and sacrifice peace offerings.

Question: What did the people of Israel say when Samuel asked if he had ever wronged them?
Response: The people of Israel told Samuel he had not defrauded or oppressed them or taken anything from anyone.

Question: Why did Samuel tell the people of Israel to present themselves before Yahweh?
Response: He told them to present themselves so he could make the case against them that they should not have asked for a king instead of trusting Yahweh to rescue them.

Question: What did God do to the ancestors of Israel after they forgot Yahweh their God?
Response: Yahweh sold them into the hand of Sisera, the Philistines, and the king of Moab, who all fought against them.

Question: What did Yahweh do after Israel’s ancestors cried out to him and asked to be rescued from their enemies?
Response: Yahweh sent Jerubbaal, Bedan, Jephthah, and Samuel to rescue them.

Question: What did Samuel remind the people of Israel that they had requested?
Response: Samuel reminded the people that they had wanted a king to reign over them, even though Yahweh their God was their king.

Question: What choice did Samuel present to the people of Israel?
Response: They could fear Yahweh and serve him, or they could rebel against his command and his hand would be against them.

Question: What did Samuel challenge the people of Israel to do?
Response: Samuel challenged the people to stand and see the great thing Yahweh is doing before their eyes.

Question: What did Samuel ask Yahweh to send so that the people would know the extent of their wickedness?
Response: He asked Yahweh to send thunder and rain during the wheat harvest.

Question: How did Samuel respond when the people of Israel realized their sin?
Response: Samuel told the people not to be afraid, but to serve Yahweh with all their heart and not turn aside.

Question: What message of comfort did Samuel give to the people of Israel?
Response: Samuel comforted the people by telling them that Yahweh would not forsake them, because of his great name.

Question: What did Samuel promise to do for Israel?
Response: He promised to continue to pray for them and to instruct them in the good and right way.

Question: What did Samuel challenge the people of Israel to do?
Response: Samuel told them to fear Yahweh and serve him faithfully with all their heart, considering the great things he had done for them.

Question: What did Saul do with the soldiers he did not choose to be with him?
Response: Saul sent the rest of the soldiers home to their own tents.

Question: How did Israel make itself stink among the Philistines?
Response: They made themselves stink among the Philistines because Jonathan had struck the garrison of the Philistines at Geba.

Question: How did the Philistines respond to the attack by Jonathan?
Response: They gathered 30,000 chariots, 6,000 horsemen, and people as numerous as the sand on the seashore to fight against Israel.

Question: How did the men of Israel react to the forces of the Philistines?
Response: They were distressed, so they hid in caves, thickets, cliffs, pits, and cisterns, and some fled across the Jordan.

Question: What did Saul do when Samuel did not come to Gilgal after seven days?
Response: Saul offered the burnt offering himself.

Question: What excuse did Saul give Samuel for not waiting for him to offer the sacrifices?
Response: He said he saw that the Philistine army was going to attack, and he had not yet asked Yahweh to help Israel, so he forced himself to offer the burnt offering.

Question: How did Samuel rebuke Saul?
Response: Samuel told Saul that he had acted foolishly by not keeping the command that Yahweh gave him.

Question: What did Samuel say would be the consequences of Saul’s disobedience?
Response: Because Saul did not obey Yahweh’s command, none of his descendants will become king. Instead, Yahweh has found someone else who will do what he wants him to do, and he will make that person the next king of Israel.

Question: What strategy did the Philistines use against the people of Israel?
Response: The Philistines sent out soldiers in three groups to attack different Israelite towns.

Question: What problem did the Israelites face in their fight against the Philistines?
Response: There were no blacksmiths in all the land of Israel, so they could not make swords or spears.

Question: How were the soldiers of Israel hindered on the day of battle?
Response: None of the people with Saul and Jonathan had a sword or spear in their hand.

Question: What secret plan did Jonathan, the son of Saul, have?
Response: Jonathan planned to cross over to the Philistines’ garrison, but he did not tell his father.

Question: What did the six hundred men with Saul not know?
Response: They did not know that Jonathan had gone.

Question: What proposal did Jonathan present to his young armor-bearer?
Response: Jonathan proposed that they cross over to the garrison of the uncircumcised, because perhaps Yahweh would act for them.

Question: What was the armor-bearer’s response to the proposal?
Response: Jonathan’s armor-bearer told him to do all that was in his heart and that he was with him and would help him in all that he wanted to do.

Question: What would be the sign to Jonathan and his armor-bearer that Yahweh had given the Philistines into their hand?
Response: If the Philistines told them to come up to them, that would be the sign.

Question: What did Jonathan say to his armor-bearer after the Philistines told them to come up?
Response: He told his armor-bearer to follow him because Yahweh had given the Philistines into the hand of Israel.

Question: What was the result of the attack that Jonathan and his armor-bearer made on the Philistine garrison?
Response: Jonathan and his armor-bearer killed about twenty men.

Question: What did Saul do when his watchmen saw the Philistine army dispersing?
Response: Saul had the people counted to see who was missing, and they discovered it was Jonathan and his armor-bearer.

Question: Why did Saul tell Ahijah the priest to bring the Box of God to him?
Response: Saul wanted to inquire of God, but he stopped because the tumult in the Philistine camp was increasing.

Question: Who rejoined the Israelites who had remained with Saul and Jonathan?
Response: The Hebrews who had previously deserted to the Philistines in their camp rejoined the Israelites. Now those men helped Saul and Jonathan and the other Israelite soldiers fight against the Philistines.

Question: Who saved Israel that day?
Response: Yahweh saved Israel that day.

Question: What caused the men of Israel to be distressed and faint?
Response: Saul had put the people under an oath, cursing any man who ate food before evening.

Question: How did Jonathan unknowingly break the oath?
Response: Jonathan, who had not heard the oath, ate some honey from a honeycomb.

Question: How was Jonathan critical of the oath his father had made?
Response: Jonathan said that if the people had been allowed to eat from the plunder of their enemies, the slaughter among the Philistines would have been greater.

Question: How did the people of Israel sin against Yahweh after their battle against the Philistines?
Response: The people ate meat with the blood still in it.

Question: What did Saul believe was the reason why he did not receive an answer when he inquired of God?
Response: Saul believed that God did not answer him on that day because someone had sinned.

Question: How did Saul say he would deal with the sin that had been committed?
Response: He said that the person who committed the sin would surely die, even if it was his son Jonathan.

Question: How was it determined that Jonathan was the person who had sinned?
Response: The priest threw a lot, and it indicated that either Jonathan or Saul had sinned, not one of the other Israelite soldiers. He threw the lot again, and the lot indicated that Jonathan had sinned.

Question: How was Jonathan saved from death?
Response: The people redeemed Jonathan and would not allow him to die because he had brought about a great salvation for Israel.

Question: How did Saul lead Israel as their king?
Response: Saul fought against all his enemies on every side and rescued Israel from the hands of those who plundered them.

Question: Why did Yahweh want Saul to attack and completely destroy Amalek?
Response: Yahweh wanted to punish Amalek for what he had done to Israel when he opposed them on the way up from Egypt.

Question: Why did Saul tell the Kenites to depart from the Amalekites?
Response: He warned the Kenites to move away because they had shown kindness to the people of Israel when they came up from Egypt. He did not want to kill them when he killed the Amalekites.

Question: How did Saul disobey the directions that Yahweh had given to him?
Response: Saul spared King Agag and the best of the sheep, cattle, and lambs, and was not willing to devote them to destruction as Yahweh had commanded.

Question: Why did Yahweh regret that he had made Saul king?
Response: Yahweh regretted making Saul king because Saul had turned from following him and was no longer obedient to him.

Question: What lie did Saul tell Samuel?
Response: Saul told Samuel that he had obeyed what Yahweh told him to do.

Question: How did Samuel respond to the lie that Saul had told him?
Response: Samuel asked about the bleating of sheep and the lowing of cattle that he was hearing.

Question: What excuse did Saul give to Samuel to explain his disobedience?
Response: He said the people had spared the best of the sheep and cattle so they could sacrifice them to Yahweh.

Question: How did Samuel rebuke Saul?
Response: He told Saul that Yahweh had sent him to devote the sinners, the Amalekites, to destruction, but instead he had swooped down on the plunder and done evil.

Question: What excuse did Saul repeat to Samuel?
Response: Saul insisted that he had obeyed, but that the people brought back the best of the plunder to sacrifice to Yahweh at Gilgal.

Question: What was Samuel’s response to Saul?
Response: Samuel said that to obey is better than sacrifice, and that rebellion is as the sin of divination, and because Saul rejected Yahweh’s word, Yahweh rejected him as king.

Question: What reason did Saul give Samuel for his sin?
Response: Saul said he had disobeyed Yahweh’s command because he was afraid of the people, and so he listened to their voice.

Question: What declaration did Samuel give to Saul from Yahweh?
Response: Samuel said that Yahweh had rejected Saul from being king over Israel because Saul had rejected the word of Yahweh.

Question: To whom was Yahweh going to give the kingdom of Israel?
Response: Samuel told Saul that Yahweh was going to give the kingdom to one of Saul’s neighbors, an Israelite who was a better man than Saul.

Question: What did Samuel do to finish the task that Saul had not done?
Response: Samuel hacked King Agag to pieces before Yahweh.

Question: Why did Yahweh tell Samuel to stop mourning for Saul?
Response: Yahweh told him to stop because he had rejected Saul from being king over Israel.

Question: What did Samuel fear would happen if Saul heard he was going to Bethlehem?
Response: He feared that Saul would kill him.

Question: How did Yahweh say that Samuel would know whom to anoint?
Response: Yahweh told Samuel that he would show him what to do and that Samuel should anoint the one Yahweh told him to anoint.

Question: How did the elders of Bethlehem act when Samuel arrived?
Response: They came trembling to meet him and asked if his coming was in peace.

Question: What did Samuel think when he looked at Eliab?
Response: He thought that surely Yahweh’s anointed one was standing before him.

Question: How did Yahweh explain that he does not see as a human sees?
Response: Yahweh said that a human looks on the outward appearance, but Yahweh looks on the heart.

Question: What did Samuel say to Jesse after seven of his sons passed before him?
Response: Samuel said that Yahweh had not chosen any of them.

Question: Where was Jesse’s youngest son before Samuel called for him?
Response: The youngest son was out in the pasture tending the flock of sheep.

Question: What happened to David after Samuel anointed him?
Response: The Spirit of Yahweh rushed upon David from that day forward.

Question: What spirit troubled Saul after the Spirit of Yahweh had turned away from him?
Response: A bad spirit from Yahweh terrified him.

Question: What did Saul’s servants suggest a skillful player on the lyre could do for him?
Response: When the bad spirit was on Saul, the person would play the lyre, and would calm down.

Question: Which of Jesse’s sons did Saul ask to have sent to him?
Response: Saul asked Jesse to send his son David, who was with the flock.

Question: What assignment did Saul give David because he loved him greatly?
Response: Saul made David his armor-bearer.

Question: What would happen to Saul when David played the lyre?
Response: The bad spirit would turn away from Saul, and he would calm down and feel better.

Question: Why had Saul and the men of Israel encamped in the valley of Elah?
Response: They had drawn up in battle array to fight the Philistines.

Question: What did Goliath have on his head?
Response: He had a helmet of bronze on his head.

Question: What did Goliath carry between his shoulders?
Response: He had a curved sword of bronze between his shoulders.

Question: What did Goliath call the ranks of Israel?
Response: Goliath called them servants of Saul.

Question: What did Goliath say would happen to the Philistines if Israel’s chosen man killed him?
Response: He said the Philistines would become servants to Israel.

Question: When Saul and all Israel heard what the Philistine said, how did they feel?
Response: They lost their courage and became terrified.

Question: Which of Jesse’s sons had followed Saul to the battle?
Response: The three oldest sons of Jesse had followed Saul to the battle.

Question: For how long and how often did the Philistine champion present himself for battle?
Response: He presented himself for forty days, morning and evening.

Question: What was the army doing as David arrived at the camp?
Response: The army was going out to the battle array, shouting the war cry.

Question: As David spoke with his brothers, who came out from the ranks of the Philistines?
Response: As David spoke, the champion, Goliath, the Philistine from Gath, came out from the ranks of the Philistines.

Question: What would the king do for the man who killed Goliath?
Response: The king would enrich the man with great wealth, give him his daughter in marriage, and no longer require that man’s family to pay taxes or do unpaid work for the Israelite kingdom.

Question: What did David say the man who killed the Philistine would take away from Israel?
Response: The man who killed the Philistine would turn away the disgrace from Israel.

Question: What did Eliab accuse David of having in his heart?
Response: Eliab accused David of presumption and evil in his heart, saying David’s motive for coming was to watch the battle.

Question: What did David tell Saul he would do?
Response: David told Saul that he would go and fight with the Philistine.

Question: What did David do to a lion or a bear that took a sheep from the flock?
Response: He went out after it, struck it, and delivered the sheep from its mouth; if it rose against him, he would seize its jaw and strike it and kill it.

Question: Why did David say this uncircumcised Philistine would be like the lion or the bear he had killed?
Response: The Philistine would be like them because he had taunted the arrayed armies of the living God.

Question: Why was David not able to walk in the armor the king provided?
Response: He was not able to walk in the armor because he was not used it.

Question: Why did the Philistine despise David when he saw him?
Response: He despised David because he was only a youth, ruddy, and with a boyish appearance, and Goliath believed he could easily defeat David.

Question: When the Philistine came with a sword, spear, and curved sword, in whose name did David say he came?
Response: David said that he came in the name of Yahweh of Armies, the God of the armies of Israel, whom the Philistine had taunted.

Question: What did David say all the earth would know after he defeated the Philistine?
Response: All the earth would know that the Israelite people worship an all-powerful God.

Question: What did David do when the Philistine drew near to meet him?
Response: David hurried and ran toward the battle line to meet him.

Question: What did the Philistines do when they saw that their warrior was dead?
Response: The Philistines fled.

Question: What did the people of Israel do when they returned from chasing the Philistines?
Response: The people of Israel plundered the Philistine camp.

Question: What did David have in his hand when Abner brought him before Saul?
Response: David had the head of the Philistine in his hand.

Question: How strongly was the soul of Jonathan bound to the soul of David?
Response: The soul of Jonathan was bound with the soul of David, and Jonathan loved him as his own soul.

Question: What did Jonathan give to David along with his garments, sword, bow, and belt?
Response: Jonathan stripped himself of the robe that he was wearing and gave it to David.

Question: Over whom did Saul set David?
Response: Saul set him over the men of war.

Question: What had the singers’ song ascribed to David that displeased Saul?
Response: They ascribed to David myriads, but they had ascribed only thousands to Saul.

Question: What did Saul think as he threw the spear at David?
Response: Saul thought that he would strike David and pin him to the wall.

Question: Why did David succeed in all his ways?
Response: David succeeded because Yahweh was with him.

Question: What did Saul hope would happen to David if he fought the Philistines?
Response: Saul hoped the Philistines would kill David.

Question: What did Saul think his daughter Michal would be for David if Saul gave her to him?
Response: Saul thought she could be a snare for David.

Question: How did Saul command his servants to speak with David about becoming the king’s son-in-law?
Response: He commanded his servants to speak with David privately.

Question: Why did David think he was not worthy to be the king’s son-in-law?
Response: He said he was a poor and insignificant man.

Question: What was the only bride-price the king desired from David?
Response: The king desired one hundred foreskins of the Philistines.

Question: What did David and his men do that exceeded the king’s requested bride-price?
Response: David and his men struck two hundred Philistines and brought their foreskins to the king.

Question: How great was David’s success, so that he earned an excellent reputation?
Response: David was more successful than any of Saul’s other army commanders.

Question: Why did Jonathan tell David to be on his guard and hide himself?
Response: Jonathan told him to be very careful because his father was seeking to kill David.

Question: What did Jonathan tell Saul that David’s deeds had been for him?
Response: Jonathan told Saul that David’s deeds had been very good for him.

Question: What did Saul swear after listening to Jonathan?
Response: Saul swore that as Yahweh lives, David would not be killed.

Question: What happened when David again went out and fought with the Philistines?
Response: David’s soldiers killed many of the Philistine soldiers, and as a result, the rest of the Philistine army ran away.

Question: Why did Saul send messengers to David’s house to watch him?
Response: Saul wanted them to kill David in the morning.

Question: How did Michal help David to flee and escape?
Response: Michal lowered David out a window since soldiers were watching the door.

Question: What did Saul plan to do when he ordered his messengers to bring David to him in the bed?
Response: Saul planned to kill him.

Question: What did Michal use at the head of the idol in the bed to make it look like David?
Response: She put a weaving of goats’ hair at its head.

Question: When David fled and escaped, to whom did he go in Ramah?
Response: David went to Samuel in Ramah.

Question: What happened to Saul’s messengers when they saw the group of prophets?
Response: The Spirit of God came on them, and they also prophesied.

Question: After sending messengers two more times, what did Saul do?
Response: Saul himself went to Ramah.

Question: How long did Saul lie naked before Samuel?
Response: He lay naked all that day and all that night.

Question: What did Jonathan say his father did not do without telling him?
Response: He said that his father did nothing either great or small without revealing it to him.

Question: How close did David say he was to death?
Response: He said that there was only a step between him and death.

Question: What did David ask Jonathan to let him do until the third evening?
Response: David asked to hide in the field until the third evening.

Question: What reason did David tell Jonathan to give Saul if Saul missed him?
Response: He was to tell Saul that David had asked make a brief visit to his city, Bethlehem, for the yearly sacrifice for his whole clan.

Question: Why did David ask Jonathan to deal faithfully with him?
Response: David asked this because Jonathan had brought him into a covenant of Yahweh.

Question: Where did Jonathan ask David to go so they could talk?
Response: He asked David to go with him out into the field so they could speak privately.

Question: Why did Jonathan want to make his father’s intentions known to David?
Response: Jonathan promised to reveal his father’s intentions so that David flee.

Question: How did Jonathan know that David would keep his promise that he would not harm Jonathan’s descendants?
Response: Jonathan knew that David would keep that promise because David loved Jonathan as much as he loved himself.

Question: Whom did Jonathan say he would send to find the arrows he shot?
Response: He said he would send a boy to find the arrows.

Question: When the festival of the new moon came, what did the king do?
Response: The king came to the table for the feast.

Question: What did Saul think might have happened to David to make him absent from the feast?
Response: Saul thought it was an accident and that David was not ritually clean.

Question: Why did Jonathan say David had to attend his family’s sacrifice?
Response: He said David’s brother had commanded him to be there.

Question: What did Saul call his son Jonathan in his burning anger?
Response: He called him the son a mother who must have been unfaithful to Saul, because no real son of his would have done that.

Question: Why was Jonathan grieved for David?
Response: He was grieved for David because his father had dishonored him, saying unfairly that David was not loyal to him.

Question: Where did Jonathan shoot an arrow as the boy ran?
Response: Jonathan shot an arrow over the boy so that it would land on the ground far ahead of him.

Question: What did Jonathan do after David left?
Response: Jonathan went back into the city of Gibeah.

Question: What did David say the king had told him to let no one know?
Response: He said the king had commanded him to let no one know anything about what the king sent him to do.

Question: What did the priest tell David about the bread he had?
Response: The priest said that there was no common bread, but there was holy bread, if the young men not recently become ceremonially unclean by having sexual relations with women.

Question: From where had the bread of the Presence been taken?
Response: The bread of the Presence had been taken from the table in Yahweh’s presence in the sacred tent.

Question: What was the work of Doeg the Edomite, who was there that day and saw what Ahimelech did to hel David?
Response: Doeg the Edomite was the chief of the herders who worked for Saul.

Question: Why did David say he had no weapons with him?
Response: He said that he had not brought any weapons because the king’s matter was so urgent that he did not have time to get any weapons.

Question: From whom was David fleeing when he went to Achish, the king of Gath?
Response: He was fleeing that day from Saul.

Question: What did David pretend to be when he changed his behavior before Achish and his servants?
Response: He pretended to be insane whenever he was with the officers or the king.

Question: What did Achish ask his servants when he saw that David was acting as if he was insane?
Response: He asked them why they had brought a raving man to him.

Question: Who gathered with David at the cave of Adullam?
Response: Everyone who was in distress, in debt, or who did not like what was happening in Israel with Saul as king.

Question: How long did David’s father and mother stay with the king of Moab?
Response: They stayed with the king the whole time that David was in the stronghold.

Question: Who did Saul hear had been discovered?
Response: He heard that David and the men who were with him had been discovered.

Question: What did Saul accuse his servants of not revealing to him?
Response: Saul said that none of his servants had revealed to him that his own son had made a covenant with David or had incited David against him.

Question: What two things did Doeg the Edomite tell Saul that Ahimelech gave to David?
Response: Doeg the Edomite told Saul that Ahimelech gave David provisions and the sword of Goliath the Philistine.

Question: What did Saul say Ahimelech did for the son of Jesse besides giving him bread and a sword?
Response: Saul said that Ahimelech had inquired of God for him.

Question: What were the servants of King Saul not willing to do?
Response: They were not willing to attack the priests of Yahweh.

Question: Whom did Doeg kill with the sword in Nob, the city of the priests?
Response: He killed men and women, children and infants, and oxen, donkeys, and sheep.

Question: Where did Abiathar go when he escaped the killing?
Response: Abiathar ran away and joined David.

Question: For what did David tell Abiathar he was responsible?
Response: David said that the death of every person in Abiathar’s father’s house was his fault.

Question: About what did David inquire of Yahweh when he heard the Philistines were robbing the threshing floors at Keilah?
Response: David asked Yahweh if he should go and strike the Philistines.

Question: Why did David inquire of Yahweh again about the Philistines?
Response: David’s men told him they were afraid even in Judah, and would be much more afraid to go to Keilah against the Philistine armies.

Question: What was the outcome when David and his men fought with the Philistines?
Response: David struck the Philistines with a great blow, drove off their livestock, and saved the people of Keilah.

Question: Why did Saul think he could capture David and his men in Keilah?
Response: Saul thought God had delivered David into his hand because David had shut himself in a city that had gates and bars and locks to keep David from leaving.

Question: After David knew that Saul was plotting harm against him, what did David say to Abiathar the priest?
Response: David told Abiathar the priest to bring the ephod so they could use it to consult Yahweh.

Question: What did David ask Yahweh concerning Saul and the men of Keilah?
Response: David asked if Saul would come down to Keilah and if the leaders of Keilah would surrender him into Saul’s hand.

Question: What was Yahweh’s reply to David concerning the men of Keilah?
Response: Yahweh told David that the men of Keilah would surrender him to Saul.

Question: Why did Saul stop his pursuit of David at Keilah?
Response: Saul was told that David had left Keilah, so he stopped his pursuit.

Question: How did Jonathan, Saul’s son, help David in the wilderness of Ziph?
Response: He went to David and reassured him that he could trust God to protect him.

Question: Before Jonathan went home, what did he and David do in the forest?
Response: With Yahweh as their witness, David and Jonathan repeated their solemn promise to be loyal to each other.

Question: Why did David and his men go to the wilderness of Maon?
Response: Someone told David that Saul and his men went to search for David so he and his men went farther south to the wilderness of Maon.

Question: What happened as Saul and his men were surrounding David and his men?
Response: A messenger came to Saul and told him that the Philistines had made a raid against the land.

Question: What was Saul’s response to this news?
Response: Saul returned from pursuing David and went to meet the Philistines.

Question: What did Saul do when he heard that David was in the wilderness of En Gedi?
Response: He took three thousand chosen men and went there to seek David and his men.

Question: What did David do to Saul in the cave instead of killing him?
Response: David secretly cut off the edge of Saul’s robe.

Question: Why did David not allow his men to attack Saul?
Response: David’s heart struck him, and he said he would not stretch out his hand against his master, because he was the anointed of Yahweh.

Question: When David left the cave after Saul, how did he show respect to Saul?
Response: David called out to Saul as his master the king, and then he bowed with his face to the ground.

Question: How did David prove to Saul that he did not want to harm him?
Response: David showed Saul the edge of his robe that he had cut off, proving he had been close enough to kill him, but did not.

Question: What was Saul’s response to David?
Response: He asked if it was David’s voice, called him his son, then lifted up his voice and wept.

Question: Why did Saul say that David was more righteous than he was?
Response: David had repaid Saul with good even though Saul had done evil to David.

Question: What did Saul say he knew about David at this time?
Response: Saul knew that David would surely be king and that the kingdom of Israel would be established in David’s hand.

Question: Before Saul went home, what oath did David swear to him?
Response: David swore that he would not cut off Saul’s descendants or destroy his name from his father’s house.

Question: What kind of man was Nabal in his dealings?
Response: He was harsh and evil in his deeds.

Question: What was Nabal’s wife Abigail like?
Response: She had good insight and was beautiful in appearance.

Question: Whom did David send to greet Nabal while he was shearing his sheep?
Response: David sent ten young men to greet Nabal.

Question: Why did David think that Nabal would be generous to him and his men?
Response: David’s men had not harmed Nabal’s shepherds, and nothing of theirs was missing while they were near David’s men.

Question: What did David’s young men ask Nabal to give them?
Response: They asked Nabal to please send back with them something from the feast that he prepared for this occasion.

Question: What was Nabal’s response to David’s young men?
Response: Nabal spoke of disrespectfully of David, implying that he was a runaway slave wandering the countryside, and he said he would not give any of his food and water to them.

Question: What did David tell his men to do when he heard Nabal’s response?
Response: David ordered his men to strap on their swords.

Question: When one of the young men spoke to Abigail, how did he say that David and his men had treated Nabal’s shepherds?
Response: He said the men were very kind to them and did not harm them or steal anything from them.

Question: Why did the young man go to Abigail instead of to Nabal?
Response: He said that Nabal is such a wicked man that he will not listen to anyone who tries to tell him what he should do.

Question: What did Abigail do after hearing about her husband’s response?
Response: Abigail hurriedly gathered provisions, put them on donkeys, and told her young men to go ahead of her, taking them to David, but she did not tell Nabal.

Question: Whom did Abigail meet as she rode her donkey down the mountain?
Response: David and his men were coming down to meet her, and she encountered them.

Question: What had David said he planned to do after protecting Nabal’s possessions in vain?
Response: David had said that by morning he would kill Nabal and every male person in his household.

Question: What did Abigail do when she met David?
Response: Abigail hurriedly got down from her donkey, respectfully bowed to the ground before David, accepted the blame for the way he was mistreated, and asked to speak to him.

Question: What did Abigail hope to prevent by speaking to David?
Response: She hoped to keep David from killing many people himself instead of relying on Yahweh to protect him.

Question: What did Abigail ask David to do with the blessing she brought?
Response: Abigail asked that the blessing be given to the young men who followed David and that he would forgive her transgression.

Question: Who did Abigail say would make David to be king?
Response: Abigail said that Yahweh would make David king of Israel and then allow many of his descendants to become king after him. because he was fighting the battles of Yahweh.

Question: Who does Abigail say will sling away the lives of David’s enemies?
Response: She says that Yahweh, David’s God, will sling them away as swiftly as a stone flies from the pocket of a sling.

Question: Whom did David say had sent Abigail to meet him?
Response: David said that Yahweh, the God of Israel, had sent Abigail.

Question: Why did David say that Abigail and her discernment were blessed?
Response: Because Abigail had kept him that day from entering into bloodshed and from saving himself with his own hand.

Question: Who had kept David from harming Nabal’s household?
Response: Yahweh, the God of Israel, had restrained David from harming them.

Question: Why did Abigail not say anything to Nabal when she returned from seeing David?
Response: Nabal was holding a feast like a king’s feast and was very drunk.

Question: What happened to Nabal in the morning when Abigail told him what had happened?
Response: He had a stroke and could no longer move; about ten days later, Yahweh struck Nabal and he died.

Question: What did David do when he heard that Nabal was dead?
Response: David sent messengers to speak with Abigail, to take her as his wife.

Question: What did Abigail do when she heard David wanted to marry her?
Response: Abigail hurriedly arose, rode on a donkey with her five young women, and followed David’s messengers to become his wife.

Question: What had happened to David’s wife, Michal?
Response: Saul had given her to another man as his wife.

Question: Whom did Saul take with him to seek David in the wilderness of Ziph?
Response: Saul took three thousand chosen men of Israel.

Question: How did David know that Saul was coming after him into the wilderness?
Response: David sent out spies and learned for certain that Saul had come.

Question: Where did David go after learning that Saul had come?
Response: David arose and went to the place where Saul had camped.

Question: What did Abishai want to do when he and David saw Saul sleeping inside the camp?
Response: Abishai wanted to strike Saul with his own spear and pin him to the earth.

Question: Why did David not want Abishai to kill Saul?
Response: David said no one could kill Yahweh’s appointed king and be innocent. He said that Yahweh will surely punish anyone who kills him.

Question: How did David say Saul must die?
Response: David said that Yahweh may cause him to die, or he may die of some disease or of old age, or enemy soldiers may kill him in a battle, but David would not kill him.

Question: What did David take from beside Saul’s head?
Response: David took the spear and the jug of water from near Saul’s head.

Question: Where did David go after he left Saul’s camp?
Response: He went across a valley and stood on the top of the hill far away from Saul’s camp.

Question: Why did David say Abner, the man who was to watch over Saul, deserved to die?
Response: He said Abner deserved to die because he did not watch over his master, the anointed of Yahweh.

Question: What question did David ask Saul?
Response: David asked why his master was pursuing David, his servant, and what evil he had done.

Question: What did David say should happen to the people who had caused Saul to be angry with him?
Response: David said that they should be cursed before Yahweh because they had driven him out from the inheritance of Yahweh.

Question: What did Saul say to David?
Response: Saul said that he had sinned, had acted foolishly, and would not harm David anymore. He asked David to come back home.

Question: What did David tell Saul to do about his spear?
Response: He told Saul to send one of his young men over to get it.

Question: What did David say about the value of both of their lives?
Response: David said that just as Saul’s life was great in his eyes that day, he hoped that Yahweh could consider his own life to be great and rescue him from every danger.

Question: Why did David think that he needed to escape into the land of the Philistines?
Response: David felt that otherwise Saul would capture him and kill him.

Question: What made Saul no longer seek for David?
Response: Saul heard that David had fled to Gath.

Question: What did David ask for and receive from Achish the Philistine?
Response: David asked for a place to live in one of the villages in the countryside, and Achish gave him Ziklag.

Question: What did David and his men do when they raided the Geshurites, Girzites, and Amalekites?
Response: They struck the land, did not let a man or woman live, and took the sheep, cattle, donkeys, camels, and garments.

Question: Where did David tell Achish he had raided?
Response: David would say that he had raided the south of Judah, or the south of the Jerahmeelites, or the south of the Kenites.

Question: From the raids, why did David not bring anyone alive back to Gath?
Response: David did this so that they would not be able to tell Achish the truth about he had really done.

Question: Why did Achish trust David?
Response: Achish believed what David told him and thought that David had made himself to be hated among his own people so that he would always have to stay and serve Achish.

Question: What did Achish want David and his men to do when the Philistines gathered to fight Israel?
Response: Achish wanted David and his men to go out with him into the camp for battle.

Question: Whom had Saul removed from the land after Samuel’s death?
Response: Saul had removed those who consulted with ritual pits and familiar spirits from the land.

Question: Where did the Philistines and the Israelites set up their camps to fight?
Response: The Philistines camped at Shunem, and Israel camped at Gilboa.

Question: Why did Saul search for a woman who was a mistress of a ritual pit?
Response: Saul was very afraid of the Philistine army, and Yahweh did not answer him by dreams, Urim, or prophets, so Saul sought a women who owns a pit that she uses to contact people who have died.

Question: What was the woman afraid of when the disguised Saul went to talk to her?
Response: She was afraid that he was setting a trap for her life, because Saul had cut off those who try to contact people who have died or to try to talk to spirits.

Question: What did Saul promise the woman?
Response: Saul swore by Yahweh that no one would punish if she did what he asked.

Question: How did the woman know that the man who asked her to bring up Samuel was Saul?
Response: When the woman saw Samuel, she cried out with a loud voice and said to Saul that he had deceived her.

Question: What did the woman say Samuel looked like?
Response: The woman said she saw an old man wearing a robe.

Question: Why did Saul say he had brought up Samuel?
Response: Saul said that he was in great danger but God was no longer helping him, so he wanted Samuel to tell him what he should do.

Question: What did Samuel say Yahweh had done with Saul’s kingdom?
Response: Yahweh had torn Saul’s kingdom from his hand and had given it to his fellow, to David.

Question: What did Samuel say would happen to Saul and his sons the next day?
Response: He said that the next day, Saul and his sons would be with him in death.

Question: What did the woman and Saul’s servants urge him to do?
Response: They urged him to eat a piece of bread to have strength to go on his way.

Question: What did the woman prepare for Saul and his servants to eat?
Response: She killed a fatted calf from the stall of her house, and she baked unleavened bread for them to eat.

Question: How did Achish defend the presence of David on the battlefield to the Philistine rulers?
Response: Achish said that in the year that David had been living near him, David had been completely loyal to him.

Question: What were the commanders of the Philistines afraid that David would do during the battle?
Response: They feared that David would become an adversary to them in the battle by turning against them.

Question: What did Achish tell David to do because the commanders of the Philistines did not approve of him?
Response: He told David to return and go in peace.

Question: What was Achish’s final decision about David?
Response: Achish said that David was good in his eyes like an angel of God, but the commanders had said David must not go up with them to the battle.

Question: What did Achish tell David to do in the morning?
Response: He told David and his men to rise early in the morning and go away as soon as it was light.

Question: What did David and his men find had happened to Ziklag while they were away?
Response: The Amalekites had raided Ziklag, burned it with fire, and taken captive all the women who were in it.

Question: How did David and his men react to what they found in Ziklag?
Response: They lifted up their voices and wept until they had no more strength to weep.

Question: Why was David greatly distressed?
Response: His two wives had been taken captive, and the people were talking about killing David by stoning him.

Question: What did David ask Yahweh?
Response: David asked Yahweh if he should pursue the band of raiders and if he would overtake them.

Question: What was Yahweh’s answer to David?
Response: Yahweh told him to pursue the men who took their families, for he would catch up with them and be able to rescue their families.

Question: What happened to two hundred of David’s men at the wadi of Besor?
Response: They were too exhausted to cross the wadi, so they stayed behind.

Question: What was wrong with the Egyptian man that David and his men found in the field?
Response: He had not eaten bread or drunk any water for three days and three nights.

Question: Why had the Egyptian slave’s Amalekite master abandoned him?
Response: His master abandoned him because he became sick three days earlier and it was difficult for him to keep traveling with the raiding band.

Question: What did the Egyptian require before he would lead David to the raiding party?
Response: He made David swear by God that he would not kill him or deliver him into the hand of his master.

Question: What were the raiders doing when David attacked them?
Response: They were spread out over the land, eating, drinking, and celebrating because of all the great plunder they had taken.

Question: What did David recover after his attack on the raiders?
Response: He rescued everything the Amalekites had taken, including his two wives.

Question: Why did the evil and wicked men not want to share the recovered plunder with those who stayed behind?
Response: They said those who stayed behind did not go with them.

Question: Why did David tell the men they should all share alike?
Response: He said Yahweh had given them what they had and had given the raiders into their hand, so the share of the one who went to battle should be the same as the one who stayed with the equipment.

Question: What did David do with some of the plunder when he came back to Ziklag?
Response: David sent some of the plunder to the elders of Judah who were his friends, telling them it was a blessing for them from the plunder of the enemies of Yahweh.

Question: What happened to the men of Israel when they fought the Philistines on Mount Gilboa?
Response: The men of Israel fled from the Philistines to Mount Gilboa, but the Philistines killed many of them there.

Question: What happened to Saul’s sons?
Response: The Philistines overtook Saul and his sons and killed Jonathan, Abinadab, and Malchi Shua.

Question: What happened to Saul in the battle?
Response: The fighting became very fierce around Saul. The Philistine archers realized that he was the king of Israel, so they aimed many arrows at him and wounded him badly.

Question: What did Saul ask his armor-bearer to do?
Response: Saul asked him to draw his sword and stab him so that the uncircumcised Philistines would not come and abuse him.

Question: What did Saul do when his armor-bearer was afraid and would not kill him?
Response: Saul took out his own sword and threw himself on it, killing himself.

Question: What did the armor-bearer do after he saw that Saul was dead?
Response: He also fell on his own sword and died with Saul.

Question: Why did the men of Israel who lived across the valley and the Jordan abandon their cities and flee?
Response: They fled because they saw that the men of Israel had fled and that Saul and his sons were dead.

Question: What did the Philistines do with Saul’s body after they found it?
Response: The Philistines cut off Saul’s head, stripped off his equipment, and fastened his corpse to the wall of Beth Shan.

Question: What did the valiant men of Jabesh Gilead do after they heard what happened to Saul?
Response: They marched all night and took the corpses of Saul and his sons from the wall of Beth Shan, brought them to Jabesh, and burned them there.

Question: What did the inhabitants of Jabesh do with the bones of Saul and his sons?
Response: They buried their bones under the tamarisk tree in Jabesh and fasted for seven days.
